commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. What to call people ... or what not to call people  (5)
2. Show that ends in disarray?  (5)(5)
3. Hot shot producer  (8)(7)
4. What's kraken?  (3)(7)
5. Bull rings?  (5)(5)


NAMES
STRIP TEASE
ESPRESSO MACHINE
SEA MONSTER
PRANK CALLS
43A. There are no bad dogs, only bad owners.

monday funday.
nice to see you @sean on a different day.
FWIW BASTE is a very real thing and was a gimme; in building costumes you baste the fabric to make sure you got the fit right, and then go ahead and make it official.
Monday's mini was more challenging than the midi.
I'm not even halfway through my first pass and I feel like I'm having deja vu.
Sean thought this Monday puzzle was on the spicier side. Jim Horne had this to say about Lynn Lempel's spicy debut entries: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/13/2026
Took me an enjoyable 23 minutes using only the horizontal clues. Spent too long before seeing PLAYTHINGS, but otherwise things went smoothly enough.
My Diary of a Crossword Fiend review: https://crosswordfiend.com/2026/07/12/monday-july-13-2026/#ny

Aah, a Lynn Lempel puzzle... how do I love thee, let me count the ways...

It's that wonderful knack she has for twisting phrases into a theme, and giving us a Monday that's breezy without sacrificing interesting entries in practically all the acrosses and downs.

A Lynn Lempel puzzle is like LOX on a toasted bagel with cream cheese. You can't go wrong with it and it never fails to satisfy!
Am I the only one having trouble figuring out the meta of the midi today?  What's with the numbers?  Almost--but not--of them are primes.  But beyond that, I'm as lost as an Easter egg.
Practically perfect Monday in just about every way, to quote Mary Poppins.
Made this harder for myself by spelling it 'HUNNY' at first but another solve with no look ups :)

The arguing actors made such a scene.

(They need to learn to playwright.)

The Wordplay column says: "Finally, a [Small aircraft for short flights] is a TURBOPROP, which is a new word for me."

The theatre word PROP stands for "stage properties." I learned this when I was in the drama club in high school. One year, I was assigned to be the "prop mistress." I had to find the stage properties such as lamps, ashtrays, books, ottomans, etc., to dress up the set.

The next year I was the assistant director for the second act.
An elegant Monday treat!

Ms Lempel (Okay if we call you Lynn?)
Made a puzzle quite RACY with SIN
With PLAYTHINGS, (some ORAL
But none really immoral)
And all served up with a grin.

I deny that the HERO’s a WEASEL
Or a Dorian Gray on an easel
And the SEXIEST COP
With a bright TURBOPROP
Leads to less woe than a poor CURR’s fleas ’l

Thank you for all the fun, Lynn!

Just noticed the picture on the column! Thanks for the Cincinnati call out, Sean. But I can assure you a Reds player would never cheat. Or if we did, we’d have a heck of a lot better record than we do now.

No constructor notes?
Honestly. This was probably the thing I was looking forward to most for a Monday. 

Also. I never really cared about this before since I only did Friday and Saturday. 
But what is the thinking on the early release of Monday?  
“Let’s cut short the time we feature the grid we’re most famous for, because solvers are just jonsing for a Monday grid”

I misread the clue for 25A as "human's earliest phrase" instead of "phase," according to Freud. And I thought, whoa, this solve is really going to have a payoff, because I sure don't know what Freud though our first few words together were. 

Tremendous puzzle, just a giant disappointment I created myself.

Here's hoping 35A and 60A were gimmes for Andrzej, our resident Chilean schoolgirl or Pakistani miner.

And pleased to see Rena Cohen elevating the elegance of today's Midi.

A turboprop is a type of engine that can be used on many different kinds of planes, some of which are quite large and long-range. The C-130 Hercules military transport plane is a turboprop. Weird clue.

36D [Singer Vikki with three Grammys] CARR Three great songs from my long-lost youth It Must Be Him https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J-qgHOQ1ofg He's a Rebel https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8S3HCG22zsk The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-j2jHOwVT4I

It's always a treat to have a Monday by Lynn Lempel.

I didn't really like the clue for TURBOPROP, because that's not really accurate any more. Except for very small operators, most airline companies have dropped the use of turboprop powered planes and switched to smaller jet planes, which are more efficient and economical. The military still has some planes for use where there are smaller runways than jets can handle, the C-130 being one of them (the Blue Angel's 'Fat Albert' support plane is a C-130). But they aren't really short hop transports. And my first airline flight (in 1963) was on a Lockheed Electra turboprop that I took from NJ to San Antonio, TX, not a short hop at all.

A little more trivia: The "Soarin'" Disney attraction was designed at home by an Imagineer and demoed for staff using an Erector Set. The Imagineer built a working model that showed how the seats for the attraction could be lifted into place for the show.

Thanks, Lynn, for the nice Monday!

Ooh, I’m clutching my pearls (with delight) at the subversive nature of the puzzle. Innocent toy PLAYTHINGS on one level, nudge nudge, wink wink RACY ERECTOR SETS just under the surface. 
I’d better fan myself with a copy of the Lady.

Lovely Monday puzzle.
I’m always a little disappointed when it isn’t spelled “hunny.”

Our columnist couldn't find another word ending in prop. May I suggest AGITPROP, in which PROP is short for propaganda? It's from the Russian and combines AGITation and PROPaganda. 
In my youth, all leftist theater productions aspired to be AGITPROP, profitable not being an option. 😏

“A day without a friend is like a pot without a single drop of honey left inside."



—Winnie-the-Pooh


Have a lovely day, friends.

Simple, clean, elegant, with a theme that lands.  That’s LL, and here it is again today.  She’s been doing this for 46 years, bringing a sea of quality to the Crosslandia pastiche.

The grid’s north took me back to my youth.  My grandparents always referred to a refrigerator as an ICEBOX.  The phrase “Holy TOLEDO!” reminded me of Batman’s Robin.  LOG CABIN was our family syrup.  And ERECTOR SETs were for me what Lego sets are for kids today.

AFRESH brought a meta-moment, as I haven’t heard that word in a long time, and when I uncovered it today, it felt new.

Your puzzles, Lynn, make me feel good through and through.  There is an art to that, and you’ve mastered it.  Thank you for another splendid outing!

Yay me! I finished it without using any of the Down clues in 20:59. A great puzzle. 

HOWEVER, i cannot agree with Sean’s assertion that malaprop would have been “a less fun entry than turboprop”! Mrs Malaprop is almost up there with the Reverend Doctor Spooner in my estimation, and definitely fun. 

Sean, your opinion is, as the great lady might have said, denuded, erogenous, and falsetto!
It’s the 21st century, people. Couldn’t we at least clue 37D as “With a reformatted floppy disk”?
A clever Monday to start the week…I also enjoyed the nod to other toys like log cabin.
All the world's an oral stage.

I took the Monday "No Downs" challenge for the first time today.  It made what would otherwise have been a ho-hum solve into a fun test of skills, (and roughly doubled the time it took to solve it).

And I still got to enjoy the excellent Monday theme.  In fact, I think I enjoyed more than I would have otherwise, since I needed all the Across-help that I could get.

For those who find Monday puzzles less-than-compelling, I encourage you to give it a try!

I played the midi for one of the first times this morning. Several clues had brackets at the end e.g., clue: “Evasive [11, 7]” and the answer was “cagey”. I googled what the brackets meant and received several conflicting answers. Then read the How to solve the crossword NYT article that’s linked in this article, but couldn’t find what these brackets mean. Could anyone help me decipher this?
A lovely Lynn Lempel puzzle to start the week! I am in a LOG CABIN right now, so 11D made me really happy.

Lynn Lempel is always so PLAYful!  She is like the Energizer Bunny of CrossWorld--just keeps going and going.
My first PLAYTHING was ..[something]CURTAIN, which was a surefire way to get smacked if you chose to hide behind onne.

Remember when everyone wanted an old ICE BOX to convert into a place to store bottles of red wine?  
TURBO PROPs are "small aircraft"?  Piper Cub  is what came to mind for me...  (My cousin's husband flew one; as a ten-year-old I  was very confused to have a cousin who was a grown woman with a baby!  Dad was the 5th of 6 boys, so his older brothers had a head-start.)

Fun Monday puzzle. Typical workout for me, and then when I was finished I had to stop and ponder for a moment, as I was thinking of a different interpretation of PLAYTHINGS. Just made for a nice 'doh' moment when I finally caught on. And my usual puzzle find - a Sunday from September 18, 1997 by Robert H. Wolfe with the title: "X Marks the Spot." Will just say that it was rather unusual - Here's a couple of theme clues and answers: "0X" NEXTTONOTHING "TH000X" AFTERTHOUGHTS Will just say that I remain puzzled even after looking at the solution. Here's the link if you want to go take a look. https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/28/1997&h=40a ....
Could someone please explain today’s Mini? I don’t understand the revealer. Maybe time for more coffee?
This was breezy and fun for a Monday! A quibble: I was taught by an expert scholar in Inca art that it is always Inca, not Incas or Incan.

How lovely! A Lynn L puzzle on a Monday, and nothing I needed to look up. It was fun, and fresh, and now I'm done, and starting my day. 

And, speaking of "everything bagels," guess what we're having for dinner? (Mentally checking the refrigerator: lox, check; cream cheese, check; Swiss cheese, check; tomato (not in fridge), check.)

I love Mondays and Fridays because they are my volunteer days at the Hospital, and hubby is in charge of dinner. But it never hurts to supply the idea and he just has to prepare it. And I spend the rest of the day at the ED imagining what it will taste like. Mmmm.
Mondays aren't about the solve. They're about the time. Today: 5:15--a new record for me.

Nice crossword, but I'm fairly certain (although I am merely a hobby aviation-fan, with limited knowledge), turboprop is a type of engine, rather than an actual aircraft, let alone one designed for shorter distances. The first turboprop engines appeared in the later stages of the second world war on the Allied side (but don't quote me on this).

Not a bad puzzle, bad day placement… This was definitely more of a Wednesday puzzle. That is not the Constructor’s fault.

Loved the theme!

Rating:
9/10
I see that the DC-10 gene is strongly expressed in the commentariat :)

Interesting late answer history search, inspired by our constructor's name. And, the string of letters...

LEMPEL 

has never appeared in any answer. 

And.... LEMPE was a part of 3 answers, but they all contained: KLEMPERER (WERNER and OTTO)

And finally - EMPEL was a part of 4 answers, all in the pre-Shortz era: 

HEMPEL, REMPEL, STEMPEL, VONKEMPELEN

Just thought that was all kind of interesting. 

....

Found this one a bit more difficult than a usual. I had never heard of Meccano or an ERECTORSET. No small plane I’ve ever taken has been a TURBOPROP nor had I even heard of one. Maybe this puzzle was just meant for someone a little older! Still finished it without having to use the checker but this struck me more as a Tuesday.

Such a clever little thing!

I hadn't thought of my erector set in decades.  Sold at a yard sale in 1970 along with my Magic 8-Ball and my Cymbal Clapping Monkey.  ... ... If I'd only known.
This definitely took me a while longer than I would have liked -- I got too focused on "toys" as a concept instead of the PLAY on words. Still, enjoyed it overall. Never heard the term "cur."

Just checking out 28A [Like the man on an annual People magazine cover] SEXIEST

The sexiest man alive for 2025 is someone I never heard of, an actor from "Bridgerton" and "Wicked." I guess we'll have to wait until November for the Sexiest Man of 2026. Any suggestions? I'm asking for a friend.

I mean besides Jamie Ding, of course.

An unusual puzzle find. A Sunday from February 4, 1968 by Anne Fox with the title "Spectrum of Songs." The grid in that one was 23 x 23 and contained 6 grid-spanning entries (all song titles) which of course never appeared in another puzzle. Those were.... RUFUSRASTUSJOHNSONBROWN SOMEWHEREOVERTHERAINBOW HASANYBODYHERESEENKELLY AKISSFROMAREDHEADEDMISS YOURETHECREAMINMYCOFFEE BRINGBACKMYGOLDENDREAMS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=2/4/1968&h=28a .....

poem made from words in today’s New York Times crossword puzzle

 
a/ a dog watches not
the transgressions of man
good dog 
good dog
a treat for the good dog
and a treat for the man
water and a treat
a/ in the empire state
on the great lake erie
and the giant fish of the great large lake
experiencing the a/ rises

Two quibbles with the clues: First, there are several aircraft that qualify as 48A that are neither small nor intended only for short flights. Second, a 53A is a denomination-specific service. There are many orher denominations that also have Christmas Eve services.

But it was still an enjoyable puzzle, all in all. Thank you!

And one more puzzle find. Thought this one was really amusing. A Wednesday from January 20, 1999 by Fred Piscop. Three straightforwardly clued answers: TINKERBELL MEDGAREVERS ILLCHANCEIT And then.... "Hint to this puzzles theme." DOUBLEPLAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/20/1999&h=26a ....
The no good dog hint is terrible. Wish I could report these fake uneducated assumptions and statements
afresh was a stretch

If the next day's game is going to be displayed at the top of the page, then the current day's game needs to be available in the "Last 7 Days" selection. You can't make us go to the archive calendar for today's puzzle!

I really disliked this Monday crossword. It seemed extremely difficult with lots of difficult words that did not seem suited for a Monday. Surprised not more people are mentioning this. Not fun at all, one of the worse ones recently.

Unfortunately, 62A [Actress EVAN Rachel Wood] has been involved in a custody battle with great actor and dancer Jamie Bell. Jamie Bell starred in the original "Billy Elliot." If anyone hasn't seen it, they should hie thee hither to watch one of the best dancing movies of all time. Filmed beautifully in the streets of a northeast England mining town, it includes some great music. Also starring Julie Walters! https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0249462/?ref_=fn_t_1 Don't go for "Billy Elliot: The Musical." That is a filming of a stage performance.

My favorite encore clues from last week:

[Double-decker checker]  (4)
[Food processor]  (7)


KING
STOMACH
@Lewis Excellent choices! Though I'm still irked at how long it took me to switch from Crankcalls to Prankcalls.

@V
Same here

@V

That is a great example of a streak breaking cross.  Virtually impossible to find--they both work quite well, and one doesn't automatically trigger thinking of the other, somehow.

@HeathieJ 
Suffix for Oc.

@Steve L
I knew that entry would inCURR some wrath, and I quite agree with you.

@Steve L
Preferably in a puzzle with the clue Suffix for Caffe.
@Steve L  agreed!

@Steve L

I have to say that really good dogs can sometimes do really bad things. My evidence: from my favorite TV show "America's Funniest Videos." Just today, there were some real doozies. I can't hardly believe them when I see what those cute puppies can do when left alone for a little while.--but soooo funny!!

@Bob T.

That REALLY sounds interesting.  I'd love to know about how to find it--if you can provide me with some without knocking yourself out.

@Steve L

Okay. I’m gonna be that guy. 

So all dogs in existence. There are no just inherently bad dogs?  Like 3 standard devs from average dog.  None?
I get that I’m taking this literally. And I get that dogs are incredibly emotional support animals. 
But please.  

I guess there are no bad kids. Just bad parents. 

Feel free to down rec me.

@Weak

I do see your point of view.  I mean, there's no more reason to believe a dog would be less liable to sudden inexplicable violence as we are.

I would imagine that in wolves, pups three standard deviations off "mean wolf" probably wouldn't get along well enough with the others to survive for very long.

Come to think of it...is the "lone wolf" really a thing?  And if so, are these simply the pest wolves that were driven off but managed to survive anyway?

@Francis

Look for the book Survival of the Friendliest by Brian Hare and (his spouse) Vanessa Woods.  And if you follow them to their other writings, you'll enter a wonderful rabbit hole of knowledge about bonobos and chimpanzees.

@Steve L

I know your comment was meant to be lighthearted. But the truth is a bit more nuanced than that.

I have spent years working with a dog who is, quite often, very bad. (This is not my Kangaroo dog, it’s the other one, who I lovingly call The Crocodile.) In the process, I have learned a lot about a dog’s emotional development. Simply put, personality forms within the first year, and traits developed in this time tend to lock in. Any abuse or neglect in this time can lead to lifelong behavioral issues that may be impossible to overcome later on.

It’s also true that some dogs are simply born with a metaphorical screw loose, just like some humans. I rescued my dog off the streets and I don’t know what made him the way he is, but I know it’s not my fault. (Or his.)

Also: both of my dogs respectfully request that the NYT Crossword knock it off with the “cur” slurs. Thank you kindly.

@Vaer

Okay, first, I get unreasonably angry at the delay between when the emus let us post consecutive comments. I know there are trolls and bots come up but it's ridiculous!

Second, @Vaer

Oh dear, sorry to yell at you! I still had my caps on for voice to text and didn't notice. 😘

@HeathieJ
No worries. I thought of you when I saw BEL, but wasn't going to mention it, so as not to be seen as nagging.  No guilt if you never get to it.

@HeathieJ
Unless you're a dog – which I beg leave to doubt, no one typing with its nose could go on at such lengths 😄 – you don't have grandpups.
Dogs are dogs. People are people. Which is not to say that they can't love each other, but they are different species.
Now go give your dog's pups a hug and a snuffle.

@Heidi
I was referring to the Current USian Regime – abbreviated as CUR – for a while but I stopped.  After all, I thought to myself, why slander perfectly respectable dogs?
@Steve L Nah there are definitely some "bad" dogs, if bad means not fit for domestic life.
@Steve L Good point. Our dog trainer said the following; " if your dog goes poo in your house, roll up a newspaper, and hit yourself on the head, with it."

@Bob T.

My mom sewed. I picked up a lot of the vocabulary (though few of the skills) at an early age. BASTE was a gimme for me, too.
@Bob T. I mixed up sew with sow (to cast seeds) and I was sew confused 😂

@Andrzej
The whole point of basting is to hold two pieces together only sufficiently to allow a tighter, longer-lasting attachment to be applied using a machine stitch, after which the line of basting is removed.

@Andrzej, this doesn’t have anything to do with this thread, but when I saw the clue “Polish for publishing,” my first thought was that it meant the Polish word for “publishing,” and I thought, “Andrzej will love this!” Which is funny because I obviously don’t know you, but I regularly read the crossword comments and always appreciate your perspective.

(Thankfully, this crossword did not require us to know any Polish!)
@Barry Ancona Nice! Very fun highlight reel of Lynn Lempel's many, many playful debuts. Thanks for sharing, Barry.

@Eric Hougland

I’m not trying to be critical. 
But the picture of your ‘lox’ is incorrectly placed. 
And I don’t get the Austin city limits link. 

I think Mondays are probably a tough write up in general.

@Eric Hougland

I don’t read that column unless you have linked it. 

So again, no shade. But I don’t see you calling out that the song was called “at once”. Maybe that’s the style of the column. But it seemed random. I don’t even know if I would have made the connection even if I knew the title of the song.

Just friendly unsolicited uneducated feedback.

Eric Hougland,

One of my (many) favourites by Spoon.

@Eric Hougland
I faintly recall erector sets as having just round wooden pieces with holes and dowels that fit into the holes. Meccano for sure did not own it then. Maybe I'm thinking of another toy that I remember my brother playing with.

@Times Rita

Tinker toys. Erector sets were metal.

@Nancy J.
Actually .... many of the songs, and singers, in the crossword are completely unknown to me!

@Nancy J.
Including Spoon

@Jane Wheelaghan

I didn't know Spoon either.  That's what I like about Fiend reviews.  I frequently find a new artist to listen to.

@sotto voce
Absolutely. Now if they'd only give us another Robyn Weintraub Friday.

@Joe Horton 

Took me a while to get it.  The answers sound like the letters of the alphabet in that order.  So (3,4) sounds like (C,D).

@Stephen
Thank you very much for the explanation!!!

@Weak

…Was a nice revealer…

@Stephen
Thank you!  I got the puzzle in normal time, but the theme and the numbers made no sense at all.  I appreciate the explanation!
@Confused Kiwi I waited until I had enough crosses to establish what spelling was intended.
@Confused Kiwi A A Milne, would be most proud of you. Christopher Robin, as well.

@NYC Traveler I also fell for the HuNnY trap, though I did so with my eyes open, and readily corrected it once it became obvious. 

I’ll agree that the alternate spelling is probably too much for a Monday, but for, say, Wednesday or later, it’d be fine. The clue as written gives enough of a hint to the Pooh-centric spelling, I think; at least, it made me consider it.

@Mike

If they can't play nice, it's curtains.

@Mike

Because what will they do when it's postscript? They have to draw the line somewhere.

@Mike

Is there a method to your madness?

@Mike

The list of folks who want to smack you is long...quite the cue!
@lucky13 Interesting! Never considered a theater "prop" as a shortening of something before, but that makes a lot of sense. Thanks for sharing.

@dutchiris

The fleas'll applaud.
@dutchiris — love it especially fleas’l - brilliant!

@Linda Jo
Yes, even without both ll's they know who they are.

@Francis

There is abundant evidence that Charlie Hustle not only gambled as a player but corked his bat as well.

@Matt

Corked his bat, he he
Almost makes you question Polish standards of academia, doesn’t it?

@SP Ironically, while the Reds may never cheat, if you’re correct, the team likely owes its first World Series championship to cheating, as they were the winners against the infamous Black Sox who threw the 1919 Series after at least eight of their players took bribes from gamblers.

@Matt

Almost?

@Weak

I hope Barry will back me up on this, but I think it's to allow the staff to go home earlier on the weekend.

@Francis in the good old days of morning and afternoon daily papers Mondays had no morning and Saturday no afternoon giving workers a weekend. Sunday in some countries is a different newspaper all together. Merging into single editions still means most of the content for Monday morning is pre prepared and thus can be released early. 

It is quaint that the crossword which is precooked at least days in advance follows this tradition.

@Weak

I think when you're the Queen of Mondays you draw a bye on constructor notes.

@Matt

They were, but orangeADE was not. I've been familiar with oranżada (or orangada/اورنجیڈ) almost all my life, but the English word I've seen for the first time today.

@Andrzej

La naranjada está muy rica.
@Andrzej For this English speaker, "orangeade" didn't seem like a real word and it took crosses for me to consider that that's what it was.

@Morgan Wick

👍🏾

@Shrike

No weirder than calling the 15-16th century empire of the Inca ancient 🤷🏽

@Shrike

I wondered about that, too, but I know nothing about planes.
In fact, I'm not entirely convinced they actually fly.  

(But I'm no flat-earther!)

@Shrike

I desperately wanted to shoehorn PUDDLEJUMPER in there but no rebus would make it fit and my computer screen has no deformable third-dimensional capacity.

Shrike,
With all the King Airs and Caravans and other [Small aircraft for short flights] out there being TURBOPROP, I thought the clue was fine. Personally, I have spent more time around the C-130, as I suspect is true for one or two other commenters.

@lucky13
I remember that in "Moonstruck," Loretta's father, Cosmo Castorini, adored Vikki Carr and sat in the dark, listening to her records.

@lucky13
With all due respect to Ms Carr, some songs belong to certain people. "The First Time..." belongs to Roberta Flack. If you forced me at gunpoint, I'd let Whitney Houston sing it too – a distant runner-up. But Ms Carr isn't even on the same planet with those two, when it comes to this song. Sorry.

@SBK in TO

Lauryn Hill does a memorable rendition of it as well.

@Matt
Thanks for the tip.

JayTee,
Lots of TURBOPROP air taxis [Small aircraft for short flights], so the clue seemed fine to me. Some solvers will know the C-130, a smaller group will remember the Electra.
What sort of PLAY THINGS are in your nightstand?

@The X-Phile
A lady never tells.

@Helen Wright

One could join the mile-high club in a TURBOPROP

@Mtmetz
The OH flies a Bulcow Monson two seater. 😇

@Helen Wright

Maybe TMI, but lets just say it wasn't a problem in high school in my dad's MGB...🫣😈
@Nic I’m glad that I’m not the only one!
@Nic I’m glad that I’m not the only one!
@SBK in TO Nice find!

@Lewis
Interesting that MECCANO was in the clue; it was the British ERECTOR set (something my dad--born 1912-- had coveted but never owned as a young lad)...he sent one to our son, who was already a serious Lego builder.  Those were actual brand names, so the clue was definitely a bit off IMHO.

MOL, The clue is fine. Erector Set (trademark styled as "ERECTOR") was a brand of metal toy construction sets which were patented by Alfred Carlton Gilbert and first sold by his company, the Mysto Manufacturing Company of New Haven, Connecticut, in 1913. In 1916, the company was reorganized as the A. C. Gilbert Company. The brand continued its independent existence under various corporate ownerships until 1990,[citation needed] when Meccano bought the Erector brand and consolidated its worldwide marketing with its own brand. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erector_Set
Isn't whether it would be a more interesting answer a question for another puzzle? malaPROP wouldn't fit the grid.

@Barry Ancona

Yes, we all realize they are different lengths. You'd have to rework the grid to fit it in, but it's a valid question for this puzzle.

@Elbridge Gerry

Sorry, no one expressly indicated they noticed that the words were of different lengths, so don’t go speaking for everyone. 

And considering that, if you tried to use MALAPROP in this puzzle, you’d need a black square where the T is, which would cut SETTLED in half, leaving a two-letter word above, which is a no-no. Plus, for symmetry, you’d have to cut out the second E of WEASELS, creating the same issue on the other side. 

So clearly, there’s no option to replace TURBO PROP with MALAPROP, even though they end similarly. 

Because of this, it’s decidedly not a valid question for this puzzle. Never in a million years could
MALAPROP fit into this puzzle unless it were an unrecognizable different puzzle.
Or to echo the theme, “With an upsidedown-shaken Etch-a-Sketch”.
@ad absurdum For those who prefer not to mouth off, anyway!

ad absurdum,

…thought the infant, and future, Rev. Roberts to himself.

@The X-Phile hooray! Congratulations! It’s a great way to stretch out the ticklish pleasure of an “easy” puzzle! I’m glad you enjoyed it.

For those who think it sounds impossible, just imagine that you have filled in enough of the Across answers that you can see a Down answer must be 
_
O
G
_
A
B
_
N

You don’t have be to be a genius to guess the interpolating letters. And so you fill them in and suddenly you’ve unlocked a bunch more Acrosses. Try it next Monday! (Or even tomorrow if you’re feeling brave!)

@The X-Phile you should try no acrosses sometimes too - Makes it more of a challenge to understand the theme since Monday themes are usually acrosses. I can usually solve in about 10-15 min this way but today was a little tough for me - I needed some of the across clues to finish.

@The X-Phile. Someone mentioned doing "No Downs" last week, so I went back in the archives to some old Mondays to give it a try. I love the challenge! I'm hooked. That's how I did today's. @Mia This is a regular frustration with the midi, they have themes but they don't have revealers and don't have a column explaining them. A lot of times when I see clues like these I expect there to be some sort of twist that makes the answers unintuitive, but these answers were what I'd expect based on the clues alone, so I was just left wondering what the brackets meant and how they related to the puzzle title. (Then again, I also needed this column to explain the theme of the main crossword, so...)

@Mean Old Lady my dad was the youngest of 12 in a very Catholic farm family in Kansas. He had a niece who was only two years younger than he. And as HIS youngest, I had very few cousins near my age, even though there were over 30 of them. 

As we’ve grown older, it’s interesting to note how those age differences matter both less and more, depending.

@Rich in Atlanta
Apologies, I didn't notice that you were puzzled about this puzzle. Looking at the samples you posted, here's how I would read them.
"X marks the spot" that the clue describes. So, in example 1, X is adjacent to the zero, i.e., NEXT TO NOTHING.
In example 2, X follows TH and some zeroes, i.e. AFTER TH + OUGHTS.
Hope this clears that up.

@Cherry Sure thing! Each of the starred clues are missing its last letter. For their answers to make sense, we have to suss out what that last letter is. 

For example, 5A's [*Dallas Cowboys stat] solves to TEXAS, because the clue is missing the letter E at the end. The Dallas Cowboys stat*e* is TEXAS.

@Cherry

Cherry, Cherry, coffee won't help, but I can explain it.

Each of the themed clues with an asterisk is missing ONE letter in the clue:

4A [*Place to find a doc] should really read--[Place to find a dock] PIER

5A [*Dallas Cowboys stat] should really say [*Dallas Cowboy state] TEXAS

6A [*Make a scar] is really [*Make a scarf] KNIT

7A [Number of letters missing from the ends of each starred Across clue in this puzzle]--the answer is ONE, i.e., one letter is missing from each clue to make it make sense with the answers

Now, if I can find my yarn and needles, I'll get back to my entrelac swatch.
